planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring with at least one growing bud, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade in spring; Kahili ginger tolerates more shade and cooler temperatures than most tropical gingers and is one of the best hedychiums for zone 7–10 gardens where some frost tolerance is needed.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; Kahili ginger is a Himalayan and Indian ginger species that produces some of the most fragrant and attractive flowers of any hedychium — in summer and early fall, dense, 12–18-inch cylindrical flower spikes emerge from the tips of the leafy pseudostems, covered with a succession of large, intensely sweet-scented, yellow flowers with prominent, vivid-red stamens and filaments that project well beyond the yellow petals, creating a striking red-and-yellow bicolor effect against the deep-green, lance-shaped, tropical foliage. The fragrance of Kahili ginger is one of the finest ginger scents — sweet, tropical, and penetrating — carried on the air for considerable distances from established plants. It is one of the hardiest of the ornamental hedychiums, tolerating frost to approximately 15–20°F in zone 7 when the rhizomes are mulched. Excellent in partial shade borders, beside water features, and in large containers moved indoors in winter in colder zones. Listed as invasive in Hawaii, where it has naturalized aggressively in wet forest areas.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; peacock ginger requires warm temperatures to sprout — be patient, as the rhizomes may take 4–6 weeks to emerge after planting. Allow the foliage to die back completely in fall before lifting and storing rhizomes if in zone below 9.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; peacock ginger is a Southeast Asian tropical ginger grown for its exceptionally ornamental foliage — the low-growing, ground-hugging leaves lie flat on the soil surface, each leaf broadly oval and elaborately patterned with a striking, iridescent, silver-green, feathered variegation over a deeper green background, creating a pattern resembling the eye-spot on a peacock feather. The leaves emerge directly from the soil with no visible stem and spread flat in an overlapping rosette that effectively covers the shaded ground beneath with a dense, elaborately patterned carpet. In summer, small, lilac-purple to pale-pink flowers emerge directly from the center of the leaf rosette, opening one or two at a time for a brief morning display, each flower beautiful on close inspection. Like other kaempferia species, the plants are summer-active and go completely dormant in winter. Excellent as a fine-textured, ornamentally patterned groundcover in partial to full shade, where the iridescent leaf marking is most vivid. Multiplies readily by rhizome division.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; Indian crocus flowers in early spring before or as the leaves emerge — the flower display occurs when the rhizomes are planted and warming soil triggers dormancy-break, often producing blooms before any foliage is visible, which is one of the most surprising aspects of this unusual ginger.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Indian crocus (tropical crocus, resurrection lily) is an Indian and Southeast Asian tropical ginger with one of the most remarkable blooming sequences of any ornamental plant — in early spring (or at the start of the warm season), before or simultaneously with leaf emergence, the plant produces large, showy, pure-white flowers with a prominent, deep-lavender to purple lip that look strikingly like large white crocuses, emerging directly from bare soil above the dormant rhizome before any leaves appear. After the brief, beautiful spring flower display, the broad, attractively patterned leaves emerge — dark-green with silver-green and darker-green feathered markings along the veins — and persist as an attractive foliage groundcover through the summer growing season. It then goes completely dormant again in autumn. The early-spring, leafless flowering sequence makes it appear to bloom from bare earth, creating a startling and beautiful garden effect. The rhizomes and leaves are used medicinally in Ayurvedic medicine. Excellent in shaded borders where the spring flower surprise is particularly appreciated.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; fingerroot grows naturally on the shaded forest floor and is one of the most shade-tolerant culinary gingers — it can be harvested as a root crop beginning approximately 4–6 months after planting when the leaves begin to die back.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fingerroot (Chinese ginger, Chinese keys, krachai) is a Southeast Asian tropical ginger widely used in Thai, Cambodian, and Indonesian cuisine, where the elongated, finger-like rhizomes (resembling a bunch of small, yellowish fingers, hence the common name) have a distinctive, slightly bitter-pungent, gingery-citrus flavor and aroma used to season curries, seafood dishes, stir-fries, and medicinal preparations. The rhizomes are harvested 4–6 months after planting, with a cluster of 6–12 finger-like secondary rhizomes branching from the central rhizome. The above-ground plant produces a compact rosette of attractive, oval, dark-green leaves with ornamental silver and green feathering similar to Kaempferia, and periodically produces small, pale-pink to white, orchid-like flowers at the leaf base. Fingerroot is medicinally important in traditional Southeast Asian medicine and is the subject of modern pharmaceutical research for its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. More shade-tolerant than most culinary gingers. In zone 10–12 it is a year-round perennial; elsewhere lift and store the rhizomes in winter.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; red button ginger blooms most freely in partial shade and is one of the most shade-tolerant of the tropical gingers — it actually produces more flowers in dappled light than in full sun, where the foliage tends to bleach and the plant may experience heat stress.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; red button ginger is a Central American tropical spiral ginger that produces one of the most continuously ornamental flower displays of any shade-tolerant ginger — the inflorescence is a compact, cone-shaped structure of tightly overlapping, bright-red, waxy, button-like bracts from which small, orange-yellow, tubular flowers emerge one or two at a time throughout the warm season. The cone structure persists and remains ornamental even between flowering periods, providing months of red-cone interest. The plant has a distinctive habit characteristic of all Costus gingers — the leaves are arranged in a precise, slightly ascending spiral around each stem rather than the flat-spreading arrangement of other gingers, creating an elegant, corkscrew-like effect that is architecturally attractive. The stems die back after flowering, with new stems emerging from the base the following season. Red button ginger performs at its best in partial shade with consistent moisture, and is an excellent understory plant for the shaded tropical garden. Excellent near water features and in moist, shaded borders in zone 9–12.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
